items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
Vague doubts haunt me once again...
Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Perfect match to the approved/reference translation. It preserves the mock-heroic register of «терзают смутные сомнения» and keeps the clipped parallelism of «У Шпака — …, у посла — …» without over-explaining. It also correctly avoids turning it into simple possession (“has”), aligning with the reference note that the humor is about the linked losses/appearances of items across eras.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: Perfect match to the approved/reference translation. The repetitive, banal politeness is preserved, maintaining the era-clash humor (Soviet social cliché in a royal setting) and Bunsha’s drunken, awkward self-introduction rhythm.

items.translation: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Perfect match to the approved/reference translation. It keeps the authoritarian performative force (“I demand”) and the comic inflation of Bunsha’s persona.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Perfect match to the approved/reference translation. It retains the elevated/archaic melancholy tone and the comedic mismatch with the mundane Soviet domestic context.

items.translation: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Perfect match to the approved/reference translation. It preserves the subject-role of “demons” and the panicked, superstitious framing of a technical mishap as sorcery.

items.translation: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Perfect match to the approved/reference translation. The mock-biblical/solemn diction (“Behold…”) is appropriate and keeps the joke’s ideological edge (religious certainty vs. modern mechanism).

items.translation: The floor is open to everyone!
items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: Perfect match to the approved/reference translation. It conveys the emcee-like command and the mass-participation energy without sounding like a literal “Everyone dance!”; the phrasing keeps the performative, party-leading vibe.

items.translation: Now, this was a good call!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Perfect match to the approved/reference translation. It captures Miloslavsky’s breezy self-satisfaction and the opportunistic “score” feeling, keeping the comic criminal charm.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: Perfect match to the approved/reference translation. Crucially, it avoids calling the eggplant spread “caviar,” preventing confusion and preserving the Soviet-satire inversion (cheap spread framed as exotic delicacy).

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: Perfect match to the approved/reference translation. It preserves the comedic list rhythm, the status-item cataloguing, and the trailing self-correction/afterthought feel that signals Shpak’s exaggeration and confusion.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Perfect match to the approved/reference translation. The calm, sing-song resignation is maintained, keeping the darkly comic undertone about psychiatric “treatment” while making it a shared absurdist refrain.

summary.interpretation: 0.00 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.00 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
